title: Donald Lynden-Bell

infobox.value: [Karl Schwarzschild Medal](/wiki/Karl_Schwarzschild_Medal "Karl Schwarzschild Medal") (1983)  [Eddington Medal](/wiki/Eddington_Medal "Eddington Medal") (1984)  [Brouwer Award](/wiki/Brouwer_Award_(Division_on_Dynamical_Astronomy) "Brouwer Award (Division on Dynamical Astronomy)") (1991)  [Gold Medal of the Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/Gold_Medal_of_the_Royal_Astronomical_Society "Gold Medal of the Royal Astronomical Society") (1993)  [Bruce Medal](/wiki/Bruce_Medal "Bruce Medal") (1998)  [John J. Carty Award for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/John_J._Carty_Award_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "John J. Carty Award for the Advancement of Science") (2000)  [Henry Norris Russell Lectureship](/wiki/Henry_Norris_Russell_Lectureship "Henry Norris Russell Lectureship") (2000) [Kavli Prize](/wiki/Kavli_Prize "Kavli Prize") for Astrophysics (2008)
[Ofer Lahav](/wiki/Ofer_Lahav "Ofer Lahav") [Somak Raychaudhury](/wiki/Somak_Raychaudhury "Somak Raychaudhury") [Simon White](/wiki/Simon_White "Simon White")
[Douglas N. C. Lin](/wiki/Douglas_N._C._Lin "Douglas N. C. Lin")

[CBE](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society")[[3]](#cite_note-3) (5 April 1935 – 6 February 2018) was a British [theoretical astrophysicist](/wiki/Theoretical_astrophysicist "Theoretical astrophysicist"). He was the first to determine that [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") contain [supermassive black holes](/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole "Supermassive black hole") at their centres, and that such black holes power [quasars](/wiki/Quasar "Quasar").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Lynden-Bell was [President of the Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/President_of_the_Royal_Astronomical_Society "President of the Royal Astronomical Society") (1985–1987) and received numerous awards for his work, including the inaugural [Kavli Prize for Astrophysics](/wiki/Kavli_Prize_for_Astrophysics "Kavli Prize for Astrophysics"). He worked at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") for his entire career, where he was the first director of its [Institute of Astronomy](/wiki/Institute_of_Astronomy,_Cambridge "Institute of Astronomy, Cambridge").
["The Astronomers"](http://www.starmen.space/astronomers). *[Star Men](/wiki/Star_Men "Star Men")*. Inigo Athenaeum Enterprise Inc. 2015. Retrieved 25 October 2016.
Evans, Neil Wyn (2020). ["Donald Lynden-Bell. 5 April 1935— 6 February 2018"](https://doi.org/10.1098%2Frsbm.2020.0008). *Biographical Memoirs of Fellows of the Royal Society*. **69**: 333–36

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

British theoretical astrophysicist (1935–2018)

| Alma mater | University of Cambridge |

Carty Award for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/John_J._Carty_Award_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "John J. | Doctoral students | [Ofer Lahav](/wiki/Ofer_Lahav "Ofer Lahav") [Somak Raychaudhury](/wiki/Somak_Raychaudhury "Somak Raychaudhury") [Simon White](/wiki/Simon_White "Simon White") |

**Donald Lynden-Bell** [CBE](/wiki/Order_of_the_British_Empire "Order of the British Empire") [FRS](/wiki/Fellow_of_the_Royal_Society "Fellow of the Royal Society")[[3]](#cite_note-3) (5 April 1935 – 6 February 2018) was a British [theoretical astrophysicist](/wiki/Theoretical_astrophysicist "Theoretical astrophysicist"). He was the first to determine that [galaxies](/wiki/Galaxy "Galaxy") contain [supermassive black holes](/wiki/Supermassive_black_hole "Supermassive black hole") at their centres, and that such black holes power [quasars](/wiki/Quasar "Quasar").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Lynden-Bell was [President of the Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/President_of_the_Royal_Astronomical_Society "President of the Royal Astronomical Society") (1985–1987) and received numerous awards for his work, including the inaugural [Kavli Prize for Astrophysics](/wiki/Kavli_Prize_for_Astrophysics "Kavli Prize for Astrophysics"). He worked at the [University of Cambridge](/wiki/University_of_Cambridge "University of Cambridge") for his entire career, where he was the first director of its [Institute of Astronomy](/wiki/Institute_of_Astronomy,_Cambridge "Institute of Astronomy, Cambridge").

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Donald_Lynden-Bell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

Lynden-Bell was born at [Dover Castle](/wiki/Dover_Castle "Dover Castle") in [Dover](/wiki/Dover "Dover"), [Kent](/wiki/Kent "Kent"), into a military family,[[5]](#cite_note-times-obit-5) as one of two children to Lachlan Arthur Lynden-Bell (1897–1984) and Monica Rose Thring (1906–1994). His father, a [lieutenant colonel](/wiki/Lieutenant_colonel "Lieutenant colonel"), fought on the Western Front and in the Middle East during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and had received a [Military Cross](/wiki/Military_Cross "Military Cross").[[6]](#cite_note-kavli-6) He had a sister, Jean Monica, who became a prominent music teacher in Canada.[[5]](#cite_note-times-obit-5)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

He attended [Marlborough College](/wiki/Marlborough_College "Marlborough College") before being admitted to [Clare College, Cambridge](/wiki/Clare_College,_Cambridge "Clare College, Cambridge") in 1953.[[8]](#cite_note-araa-8) After earning a distinction in the Mathematical Tripos,[[8]](#cite_note-araa-8) Lynden-Bell went on to doctoral studies in theoretical astronomy working with [Leon Mestel](/wiki/Leon_Mestel "Leon Mestel"), which he completed in 1960.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 1962, he published research with [Olin Eggen](/wiki/Olin_Eggen "Olin Eggen") and [Allan Sandage](/wiki/Allan_Sandage "Allan Sandage")[[10]](#cite_note-10) arguing that the [Milky Way](/wiki/Milky_Way "Milky Way") originated through the dynamic collapse of a single large gas cloud.[[11]](#cite_note-Eggen1962-11) In 1969 he published his theory that quasars are powered by massive black holes accreting material. From counting dead quasars, he deduced that most massive galaxies have black holes at their centres.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Lynden-Bell developed a theory for the relaxation of a system of particles in changing potential field known as "violent relaxation." Violent relaxation has many applications in dynamical astronomy, affecting the orbits of stars within star clusters and galaxies.[[13]](#cite_note-Lynden-Bell1967-13) Lynden-Bell is also known for the development of the theory of the gravothermal catastrophe, a phenomenon in star clusters that is the result of the negative [heat capacity](/wiki/Heat_capacity "Heat capacity") of gravitational systems. The catastrophe occurs when the core of a cluster shrinks and heats up, causing it to transfer energy to stars in the cluster's halo, leading the cluster core to collapse.[[14]](#cite_note-Lynden-Bell1968-14)

Lynden-Bell authored an influential 1974 paper with [James E. Pringle](/wiki/James_E._Pringle "James E. Pringle") about the evolution of disks around "nebular variables," which were later to become known as [T Tauri stars](/wiki/T_Tauri_stars "T Tauri stars") – an early phase in a star's [life cycle](/wiki/Stellar_evolution "Stellar evolution").[[15]](#cite_note-thewire-15) The paper predicts the signature of radiation from such disks, which is emitted primarily at [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") wavelengths where it dominates over the emission from the star.[[16]](#cite_note-Lynden-Bell1974-16) Excess infrared emission from young stars has become one of the primary methods used to identify these objects in astronomical surveys.[[17]](#cite_note-Lada2003-17)

In 1971, he became [Professor of Astrophysics (1909)](/wiki/Professor_of_Astrophysics_(1909) "Professor of Astrophysics (1909)") and later the first director of the [Institute of Astronomy, Cambridge](/wiki/Institute_of_Astronomy,_Cambridge "Institute of Astronomy, Cambridge"), when it formed from the merger of the Institute of Theoretical Astrophysics and the Cambridge Observatories in 1972.[[8]](#cite_note-araa-8)

In the 1980s, he was a member of a group of astronomers known as the 'Seven Samurai' (with [Sandra Faber](/wiki/Sandra_Faber "Sandra Faber"), [David Burstein](/w/index.php?title=David_Burstein&action=edit&redlink=1 "David Burstein (page does not exist)"), [Alan Dressler](/wiki/Alan_Dressler "Alan Dressler"), [Roger Davies](/wiki/Roger_Davies_(astrophysicist) "Roger Davies (astrophysicist)"), [Roberto Terlevich](/w/index.php?title=Roberto_Terlevich&action=edit&redlink=1 "Roberto Terlevich (page does not exist)"), and [Gary A. Wegner](/wiki/Gary_A._Wegner "Gary A. Wegner")) who postulated the existence of the [Great Attractor](/wiki/Great_Attractor "Great Attractor"), a huge, diffuse region of material around 250 million light-years away that results in the observed motion of our local galaxies.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Lynden-Bell, [Roger Griffin](/wiki/Roger_Griffin_(astronomer) "Roger Griffin (astronomer)"), Neville Woolf, and [Wallace L. W. Sargent](/wiki/Wallace_L._W._Sargent "Wallace L. W. Sargent") were in the 2015 documentary film *[Star Men](/wiki/Star_Men "Star Men")* that covered some of their professional accomplishments at their fiftieth reunion to redo a memorable hike.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

His research in the last years of his life mainly focused on astrophysical jets and general relativity.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Personal life and death

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Donald_Lynden-Bell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Personal life and death")]

Donald was married to [Ruth Lynden-Bell](/wiki/Ruth_Lynden-Bell "Ruth Lynden-Bell"), a professor of chemistry at the University of Cambridge, on 1 July 1961.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Lynden-Bell died at his home in Cambridge on 6 February 2018, at the age of 82.[[2]](#cite_note-ioa-2) He had a stroke in the months preceding his death, and never fully recovered.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Responding to news of his death, [John Zarnecki](/wiki/John_Zarnecki "John Zarnecki"), then [President of the Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/President_of_the_Royal_Astronomical_Society "President of the Royal Astronomical Society"), praised Lynden-Bell's contributions to astronomy, particularly his "incisive questions at scientific meetings and being generous in his support for others".[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Donald_Lynden-Bell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Honours")]

### Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Donald_Lynden-Bell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Awards")]

* [Karl Schwarzschild Medal](/wiki/Karl_Schwarzschild_Medal "Karl Schwarzschild Medal") (1983)[[24]](#cite_note-24)
* [Eddington Medal](/wiki/Eddington_Medal "Eddington Medal") (1984)[[25]](#cite_note-eddington-25)
* [Brouwer Award](/wiki/Brouwer_Award_(Division_on_Dynamical_Astronomy) "Brouwer Award (Division on Dynamical Astronomy)") of the [American Astronomical Society](/wiki/American_Astronomical_Society "American Astronomical Society"), Division for Dynamical Astronomy (1991)[[26]](#cite_note-brouwer-26)
* [Gold Medal of the Royal Astronomical Society](/wiki/Gold_Medal_of_the_Royal_Astronomical_Society "Gold Medal of the Royal Astronomical Society") (1993)[[27]](#cite_note-gold-27)
* [Bruce Medal](/wiki/Bruce_Medal "Bruce Medal") (1998)[[28]](#cite_note-28)
* National Academy of Sciences, [John J. Carty Award for the Advancement of Science](/wiki/John_J._Carty_Award_for_the_Advancement_of_Science "John J. Carty Award for the Advancement of Science") (2000)[[29]](#cite_note-Carty-29)
* [Henry Norris Russell Lectureship](/wiki/Henry_Norris_Russell_Lectureship "Henry Norris Russell Lectureship") (2000)[[30]](#cite_note-30)
* The first [Kavli Prize](/wiki/Kavli_Prize "Kavli Prize") for Astrophysics (2008), with [Maarten Schmidt](/wiki/Maarten_Schmidt "Maarten Schmidt")[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4)[[6]](#cite_note-kavli-6)
* Member of the [Norwegian Academy of Science and Letters](/wiki/Norwegian_Academy_of_Science_and_Letters "Norwegian Academy of Science and Letters").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

### Named after him

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Donald_Lynden-Bell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Named after him")]

* [Asteroid](/wiki/Asteroid "Asteroid") [18235 Lynden-Bell](/wiki/18235_Lynden-Bell "18235 Lynden-Bell")[[32]](#cite_note-32)
